title: Post 2I Sea Level Rise and Climate Change
display_title: Post 2I Sea Level Rise and Climate Change
wikitext:
[]: Post 2I Sea Level Rise and Climate Change             Quote Post by TB3 » Sun Oct 28, 2012 5:41 pm Was studying (and glorying in) the usefulness that is the deep realms of Evageeks, I found this in-show topographic map depicting Tokyo three and the greater Hakone area. Now along with Lake Ashi, Tokyo-3 and the valley down which the Hakone Tozan Railway runs (including the town where Shinji and Misato first meet among the Sachiel stomp-a-thon) I kept studying how the city of Odawara has become largely flooded as a result of 2I and the subsequent rise in sea levels. This left me wondering how much sea-levels actually rose, and to that end I began playing around in Google Earth. Comparing the new shoreline to pre-existing roads, railways and topographic features on the map led me to the following: So, in this case at least, the sea levels rose to somewhere between the two highlighted extremes, namely between 60 and 160 feet above the original sea-level. The extreme variance I suppose can be explained by geographic shifting or 'tipping' of Japan's geographic plate. I'd like to give major compliments to whoever drew this map, because although the variance in the new shoreline is quite wide, the subsequent developments make sense - old Odawara, almost entirely flooded, has now been converted into a harbor, with breakwaters visable on the map to turn the old coastal shelf into a safe anchorage. An equally large area has become an apparent airport. Continuing in this vein, are there any other canonical materials detailing the damage wrought by the Second Impact? For example, we know the Earth was thrown of its axis, but has anyone at GAINAX ever specified where the new poles are?

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Oct 29, 2012 1:59 am One of the effects of 3I was a major melt of the Antarctic ice, and a quick Google yields statements like "If all of the Antarctic ice melted, sea levels around the world would rise about 61 meters (200 feet)."

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Oct 29, 2012 10:34 am Though that's somewhat misleading, since the Antarctic landmass is also gone. It's possible sea levels would actually drop instead of rise under those circumstances, though the specifics depend on how much of that land is removed (i.e. if it's just the surface maybe not so bad, but if it does down to the seafloor or deeper it might be all kinds of catastrophic).

[]:             Quote Post by riffraff11235 » Mon Oct 29, 2012 11:00 am Bagheera wrote: Though that's somewhat misleading, since the Antarctic landmass is also gone. It's possible sea levels would actually drop instead of rise under those circumstances, though the specifics depend on how much of that land is removed (i.e. if it's just the surface maybe not so bad, but if it does down to the seafloor or deeper it might be all kinds of catastrophic).

[]:             Quote Post by TB3 » Mon Oct 29, 2012 11:57 am Some of the released water might also begin to refreeze - the tipping of the Earth's axis would also affect melting of glaciers, the Greenland ice cap, and wether or not any of this water would begin to form new icecaps at the altered poles. For what its worth, I found this very interesting: http://geology.com/sea-level-rise/ It's an online mod of Google Maps that allows you to simulate sea-level rise. Matching it's results to the Hakone map from Eva suggests a rise of 40 to 50 meters - try and out and see what your homes would be like post -2I - my current location is underwater and my previous home is now an island seperated from the UK mainland by a strait.

[]:             Quote Post by riffraff11235 » Mon Oct 29, 2012 12:07 pm TB3 wrote: For what its worth, I found this very interesting: http://geology.com/sea-level-rise/ It's an online mod of Google Maps that allows you to simulate sea-level rise. Matching it's results to the Hakone map from Eva suggests a rise of 40 to 50 meters - try and out and see what your homes would be like post -2I - my current location is underwater and my previous home is now an island seperated from the UK mainland by a strait.

[]:             Quote Post by TB3 » Mon Oct 29, 2012 12:11 pm Might be interesting to try and imagine life in our regions post 2I - for example, the nearest large town to me is in a sort of concave bowl formed by the hills, but the rise in sea levels would turn it into a massive area of water linked to the sea by a narrow channel through the valley - in short, a perfect deepwater port. Regarding Long Island, I'd imagine any of New York's skyscrapers not felled by the Earthquakes would end up sticking out of the waterline and becomming shanty-town slums acessable only by boat (as ADV suggested in their concept art for the Live Action concept).

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Mon Oct 29, 2012 12:51 pm riffraff11235 wrote: But wouldn't the sea level only decrease if the Antarctic landmass was actually blown off of the planet? If it was only blown away from the South Pole, it would actually add to the rise in the sea level.

[]:             Quote Post by TB3 » Mon Oct 29, 2012 1:07 pm Bagheera wrote: No, if it settled elsewhere it wouldn't add or subtract, it'd just even out. I tend to think it was annihilated though, i.e. converted to energy/wiped out. That would explain the shift in the Earth's axis, something that didn't (apparently) occur in 3I even though the phenomenon affected the entire planet. For some reason 2I was just boomtastic.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Oct 29, 2012 1:13 pm Bagheera wrote: Though that's somewhat misleading, since the Antarctic landmass is also gone.
